clean_story:
Light without sight. Skin as a camera.

The phrase "blind" often assumes total darkness, but many blind people detect light—just not with their eyes. Their skin, brain, and even bones act like backup sensors, turning photons into pressure, warmth, or timing cues.

Here’s how: The suprachiasmatic nucleus (a brain region the size of a grain of rice) tracks daylight through temperature shifts on the skin—morning sun warms one side of the face first, signaling dawn. Some blind people report "seeing" sunlight as a tingling scalp or a pulse in their temples, likely from infrared heat activating nerve endings. Others use bone conduction: vibrations from bright environments (like a sunny room) travel through their skull, creating a faint hum. A 2018 study found 70% of totally blind participants could guess room lighting with >80% accuracy by these clues alone.

Tools exploit this. The vOICe device turns camera input into soundscapes—brighter pixels play as higher pitches. Users "hear" a lamp as a sudden chirp. Meanwhile, tactile maps with heated lines let fingers trace sunlight paths in buildings, like reading Braille for shadows.

Three related subjects are synesthesia cross-wiring, animal magnetoreception, and how deaf people sense rhythm.
